HomeGlobal ShocksSingaporeRegional

Regional

Asian economies exposed to Middle East stress: financial markets and country-level monitoring

Energy supply

Overview

Price passthrough is sharply bifurcated: Vietnam (headline +5.5% YoY, core +4.7%, both at war-period highs) and the Philippines (+4.1% headline, +2.4 pp above baseline) are absorbing the shock visibly, while most Northeast Asian economies show contained or declining CPI. The clearest real-economy signal is South Korea's tanker shipping, where actual port calls and import tonnage are running 35% and 36% below counterfactual respectively — the most severe tanker gap in the region — while Thailand is the mirror-image outlier with tanker import tonnage running 101% above counterfactual, suggesting active re-routing or stockpiling. Industrial metals (nickel +30%, aluminum +25%, copper +15% vs baseline) and JKM LNG (+58%) confirm elevated upstream cost pressure that has yet to fully clear through regional CPI.

Key Drivers (5)

  • Vietnam headline CPI reached 5.46% YoY in April 2026 (+1.93 pp vs baseline, at its war-period high), with core CPI also at its war-period high of 4.66% (+1.39 pp); the Philippines headline CPI stands at 4.07% (+2.42 pp vs baseline) with core +0.81 pp — the two clearest cases of sustained energy-cost passthrough in the region.

    ⌗ rg.prices.vietnam⌗ rg.prices.philippines

  • South Korea's tanker shipping is the most disrupted in the region: actual tanker port calls are 35% below counterfactual (4w avg gap −22%), and tanker import tonnage is 36% below counterfactual (4w avg −35%), with war-period max gaps reaching −50% on import tonnage. Japan's tanker import tonnage gap is −14% (at its war-period worst), and Malaysia's tanker import tonnage runs −16% below counterfactual.

    ⌗ rg.shipping.kr.tanker⌗ rg.shipping.jp.tanker⌗ rg.shipping.my.tanker

  • Thailand is a sharp outlier on tanker flows: actual tanker port calls run 34% above counterfactual and tanker import tonnage is 101% above counterfactual (4w avg +30%), suggesting aggressive front-loading or re-routing of energy cargoes through Thai ports — consistent with Thailand's headline CPI remaining near zero despite the supply shock.

    ⌗ rg.shipping.th.tanker⌗ rg.shipping.th.total_port_calls_all_vessel_types_imports_exports

  • JKM LNG is +58% above the Nov-Dec baseline at $16.87/MMBtu (war-period peak was $22.35); nickel is +30% above baseline and at its war-period high; aluminum is +25% above baseline; copper is +15% above baseline. These elevated input costs are the primary transmission channel for future CPI pressure across energy-importing economies.

    ⌗ rg.financial_markets.jkm_lng⌗ rg.financial_markets.nickel⌗ rg.financial_markets.aluminum⌗ rg.financial_markets.copper

  • Indonesia sources 53% of its refined petroleum imports from Singapore (2024 Comtrade data) — the highest regional dependence — and its tanker import tonnage is running 3–5% below counterfactual on a 4w average, with war-period max gaps reaching −15%. Indonesia's headline CPI has nonetheless eased to 2.4% (−0.4 pp vs baseline), though the IPI data is stale (last reading December 2025, 154 days old).

    ⌗ rg.trade.fuel.regional_fuel_share_comparison⌗ rg.shipping.id.tanker⌗ rg.prices.indonesia
Data Gaps (4)
  • Indonesia IPI is stale (last observation December 2025, data_age_days=154); no war-period industrial activity reading available for Indonesia.
  • Vietnam 2024 Comtrade dependence ratios for both refined petroleum and industrial chemicals are incomplete (last observation 2023, data_age_days=855); Singapore's share of Vietnam's fuel and chemical imports may be materially understated.
  • Newcastle coal and crude palm oil baselines are null (series started within the war period), preventing delta-vs-baseline comparisons for these commodities.
  • Most CPI and IPI series are stale at 64 days (March 2026 vintage); April 2026 readings are only available for Indonesia, Vietnam, and Vietnam IPI, limiting real-time passthrough assessment for the majority of the region.

Financial markets

Overview

Regional financial markets show moderate and uneven stress: sovereign yields have risen materially in the Philippines (+102 bp vs baseline) and Indonesia (+64 bp), while Thailand (+48 bp) and Vietnam (+49 bp) are also elevated, but Malaysia and Japan remain contained. FX moves are modest in aggregate — IDR and PHP have weakened roughly 4% against the USD vs baseline, CNY is off 3.6% — but none have broken into severe dislocation territory. Gold has retreated 27% from its war-period peak of $5,294/oz and now sits only 10% above the pre-war baseline, signalling that acute safe-haven demand has faded even as underlying geopolitical risk persists.

Key Drivers (4)

  • Philippines 10Y sovereign yield has risen 102 bp above its Nov-Dec baseline to 6.86%, at its war-period high, with a 12-week trend of +87 bp — the largest yield move in the region and consistent with the Philippines' elevated headline CPI and FX depreciation pressure.

    ⌗ rg.financial_markets.sovereign_10_year_bond_yields_per_annum

  • Indonesia 10Y yield is +64 bp above baseline at 6.84%, with a 12-week trend of +50 bp; Thailand 10Y is +48 bp above baseline at 2.20%; Vietnam 10Y is +49 bp above baseline at 4.19% — three sovereigns with sustained upward yield momentum, contrasting with Malaysia (+6 bp) and Japan (+1 bp) which remain essentially flat.

    ⌗ rg.financial_markets.sovereign_10_year_bond_yields_per_annum

  • IDR has depreciated 4.1% vs USD relative to the Nov-Dec baseline and is at its war-period high (most depreciated level); PHP is 4.6% weaker vs baseline and near its war-period high. CNY has weakened 3.6% vs baseline and is near its war-period low. MYR is 4.1% weaker vs baseline. JPY and VND are essentially flat vs baseline.

    ⌗ rg.financial_markets.currency_strength_vs_usd_indexed_2026_01_01_100

  • Gold at $4,623/oz is 10% above the Nov-Dec baseline but has fallen 27% from its war-period peak of $5,294/oz (reached 2026-03-02), with a negative 12-week trend of −8.5%. The retreat from peak safe-haven levels suggests acute crisis pricing has unwound, though gold remains elevated relative to pre-war levels.

    ⌗ rg.financial_markets.gold
Data Gaps (2)
  • Sovereign yield data for China, Taiwan, and South Korea are not present in the dashboard snapshot; the yield picture is therefore incomplete for three major regional economies.
  • Philippines 10Y yield baseline is based on only 3 data points in the Nov-Dec window, making the +102 bp delta estimate less reliable than for other sovereigns.
Zoom

Inflation

Year-on-year inflation across 10 Asian economies (China, India, Indonesia, Japan, Malaysia, Philippines, South Korea, Taiwan, Thailand, Vietnam). Each chart shows headline CPI and core CPI side-by-side: headline captures the broadest pass-through of the Iran/Hormuz energy shock; core strips out food and energy to expose second-round effects.

Industrial Production

Industrial / manufacturing production across 10 Asian economies. Aligns with the Singapore Sectoral IPI format so cross-country trends can be read on a single comparable scale.

Regional dependence on Singapore — by product

Use the dropdown to switch between two product views. Industrial chemicals capture Singapore's value-add chemical exports; refined petroleum captures Singapore's role as the regional refining hub for Middle East crude. Indonesia, Malaysia, and Thailand are most exposed in both, but the magnitude differs sharply — single-digit shares for chemicals versus 30–50% for refined petroleum.

Refined petroleum imports from Singapore — annual SG shares by regional country

How dependent each regional economy is on Singapore for refined-petroleum imports — SITC 334 (gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, naphtha, fuel oil). Indirect channel through which Middle East crude disruption reaches the region via Singapore's refining hub. Ordered by 2024 share.

⌗ rg.trade.fuel.regional_fuel_share_comparison

Refined petroleum imports from Singapore — monthly levels by regional country

Monthly absolute imports of refined petroleum (SITC 334) from Singapore in SGD thousands, with the 2023–24 monthly average as a dashed reference line. Cards ordered by 2024 Singapore share, descending.

Industrial chemical imports from Singapore — annual SG shares by regional country

How dependent each regional economy is on Singapore for industrial-chemical imports — SITC 5 less SITC 51 (organics) less SITC 54 (pharmaceuticals), the chemicals subset most exposed to Middle East upstream cost pressure. Ordered by 2024 share.

⌗ rg.trade.chem.regional_chem_share_comparison

Industrial chemical imports from Singapore — monthly levels by regional country

Monthly absolute imports of industrial chemicals (SITC 5 less 51 less 54) from Singapore in SGD thousands, with the 2023–24 monthly average as a dashed reference line. Cards ordered by 2024 Singapore share, descending.

Regional shipping nowcast

Same shipping nowcast cards as the Singapore tab, shown one regional country at a time. Pick a country from the selector to switch the view. Source: IMF PortWatch satellite data, processed by the MAS shipping nowcast pipeline.

China port calls — overview

China's total weekly port calls — actual vs the counterfactual estimate of what calls would have been absent the war.

Total port calls (all vessel types, imports + exports)

Weekly aggregate of all vessel types arriving at this country's ports. Solid blue is actual; dashed amber is the counterfactual primary estimate.

⌗ rg.shipping.cn.total_port_calls_all_vessel_types_imports_exports

China shipping activity — by vessel type

Vessel-type drill-down for the two categories most directly exposed to Iran-related disruption: tankers (energy trade) and containers (general goods). Each card shows three weekly metrics — port calls (vessel count), import tonnage (cargo unloaded), and export tonnage (cargo loaded).

Exchange rates

Daily Asian currency rates against USD, rebased to 100 at 2026-01-01 so currencies with very different magnitudes can share an axis. Higher = local currency weaker vs USD. Source: yfinance.

Currency strength vs USD (indexed, 2026-01-01 = 100)

⌗ rg.financial_markets.currency_strength_vs_usd_indexed_2026_01_01_100

Bond yields

Daily 10-year government bond yields, % per annum. US from yfinance (^TNX); ASEAN-4 + Vietnam from CEIC.

Sovereign 10-year bond yields (% per annum)

⌗ rg.financial_markets.sovereign_10_year_bond_yields_per_annum

Commodity prices

Key commodity benchmarks relevant to Asian trade and energy exposure. yfinance for COMEX/ICE-listed contracts (Gold, Copper, LME Aluminum); investing.com for niche regional benchmarks (LME Nickel, FCPO MYR, JKM LNG, Newcastle coal, rubber TSR20).

Gold

COMEX gold futures front-month (USD/oz).

⌗ rg.financial_markets.gold

Copper

COMEX copper futures (USD/lb).

⌗ rg.financial_markets.copper

Aluminum

LME aluminum 3-month forward (USD/tonne).

⌗ rg.financial_markets.aluminum

Nickel

LME nickel (USD/tonne). Used in stainless steel and EV batteries; Indonesia is the largest producer and SE Asia a major refiner.

⌗ rg.financial_markets.nickel

JKM LNG

Japan/Korea Marker (USD/MMBtu). The Asian spot LNG benchmark assessed daily by Platts. Qatari LNG accounts for ~20% of global supply.

⌗ rg.financial_markets.jkm_lng

Rubber TSR20

Bangkok STR 20 (≡ TSR 20), 2nd-month FOB price, converted to USc/kg via daily FX. Asian natural rubber benchmark — Thailand leads regional production, with Indonesia and Vietnam close behind.

⌗ rg.financial_markets.rubber_tsr20

Newcastle coal

Newcastle FOB Australia thermal coal (USD/tonne). The seaborne Asian thermal coal benchmark.

⌗ rg.financial_markets.newcastle_coal

Crude palm oil

FCPO front-month on Bursa Malaysia (MYR/tonne). The global palm oil benchmark; Indonesia and Malaysia are the top exporters.

⌗ rg.financial_markets.crude_palm_oil

MAS EPG reports

Middle East Faultline Watch

ME Faultline Watch (“The Watch”) is a joint initiative by IED and FMS to identify countries most exposed to energy and/or financial stress arising from the Middle East conflict. The Watch focuses on economies with the weakest links—those facing heightened external spillovers amid limited energy and financial buffers—where shocks from higher energy prices, tighter financial conditions, or disrupted flows are most likely to translate into macro-financial vulnerabilities.

ME Faultline Watch — Philippines
